u SLIDE 1: TITLE SLIDE
"Good morning respected teachers and fellow students.
I'm C Bhuvaneshvar Reddy, roll number 1CD23CS032, and along with my teammates Aditya Pandit and Prachan Reddy, we 
are here to present our 6th semester project titled 'ProjectForge - Skill-based Team Formation and Collaboration 
Platform', developed under the guidance of Professor Arun P.
Before we begin, I'd like to thank our guide Professor Arun P for his valuable support and guidance throughout this project."
u SLIDE 2: PROJECT TITLE
"So, ProjectForge - a skill-based team formation and collaboration platform.
u SLIDE 3: CONTENTS
"Today's presentation is structured as follows:
introduction, problem statement, objectives, literature survey, research gap, proposed system, use cases, deliverables, and 
expected outcomes.
u SLIDE 4: INTRODUCTION (Problems)
"Let me start by talking about something we've all experienced as engineering students.
Academic projects are a core part of our education - they help us apply theoretical knowledge to solve real-world problems. 
But if we're honest, the process is often quite chaotic.
First, the process is largely unstructured. Finding the right project idea, forming a team, and coordinating work takes a lot of 
time that could be spent on actual development.
Students face several common issues:
One - Limited visibility into what projects are available and what skills they require. We often don't know what our 
classmates are working on or what opportunities exist.
Two - Team formation usually happens based on friendship or convenience rather than complementary skills. We end up in 
teams with our friends, which is great socially, but might not be optimal for the project's success.
Three - Collaboration is scattered across multiple applications. We use WhatsApp for chat, Google Drive for files, Trello for 
tasks, email for formal communication - this constant context switching reduces efficiency and things often get lost.
Four - There's very low transparency of individual contributions. When evaluation time comes, it's difficult for guides to 
assess who did what, and this sometimes leads to disputes within teams.
The result? Students often remain unaware of their peers' ongoing work, miss collaboration opportunities, and spend more 
time on coordination than on actual development."
u SLIDE 5: INTRODUCTION (Solution)
To address these challenges, we propose ProjectForge, a unified, project-centric platform designed to streamline the entire 
academic project lifecycle.
It enables students to discover project opportunities, form teams based on relevant skills, and collaborate within a 
centralized workspace.
The platform brings structure, visibility, and coordination into an otherwise fragmented process.
u SLIDE 6: PROBLEM STATEMENT
"Let me now formalize the problem statement.
Challenge one - No centralized platform exists for posting and discovering academic projects within an institution.
Challenge two - Difficulty finding teammates with complementary skills and aligned interests, leading to skill-mismatched 
teams.
Challenge three - Collaboration is fragmented across multiple tools like WhatsApp, email, Google Drive, and Trello, reducing 
efficiency and clarity.
Challenge four - Lack of transparency in individual contributions, which creates confusion during evaluation and can lead to 
grade disputes.
Challenge five - Inefficient project tracking over a semester. Guides struggle to monitor task completion, deadlines, and 
progress updates across different teams.
The impact of these challenges? Students often remain unaware of peers' ongoing work, interesting ideas, and opportunities 
to collaborate. This isolation reduces innovation and limits the learning experience.
Our project aims to solve all of these challenges systematically."

"Based on these challenges, we've identified seven key objectives for ProjectForge:
Objective One - Develop a centralized platform for project posting, sharing, and discovery. Students should be able to see all

u SLIDE 8: LITERATURE SURVEY (Part 1)
"For our literature survey, we reviewed 15 papers and systems across multiple categories - team matching, collaboration 
platforms, project management systems, and related technologies.
Let me walk you through some key findings.
Paper one - 'Personalized Group-Based Recommendation' from 2018 discusses group recommender systems, but it's 
designed for general item recommendations, not specifically for academic team formation.
Paper two - A web-based learning system from 2022 enables student interaction but lacks project workflow management.
Paper three - The Collaborative Filtering Survey from 2009 gives us recommender system techniques but again, not specific 
to academic projects.
[Move down the table]
Papers four and five - Node.js Project Management System and DBIT Portal - both provide centralized project tracking but 
lack intelligent matching algorithms.
[Look at audience]
The pattern we see here? Existing research focuses on either team formation or project management, but not both in an 
integrated way."
[Click to next slide]
u SLIDE 9: LITERATURE SURVEY (Part 2)
"Moving to the next set of papers...
Papers six through eleven cover various aspects - e-learning platforms, agile methodologies, project sharing systems, 
analytics dashboards, and security models.
Paper six - IEEE E-learning platform provides structured digital learning but no project collaboration features.
Paper seven - Agile methodology study discusses Scrum and Kanban workflows, which influenced our task management 
design, but it's a process framework, not a platform solution.
Paper eight - Student Project Sharing Platform from 2024 is actually quite close to what we're building, but it has limited 
advanced features and no intelligent team matching.
Papers nine, ten, and eleven focus on analytics, file storage, and security - all important components, but none address the 
complete project lifecycle we're targeting.
What's interesting is that these papers validate that pieces of our solution exist in research, but no one has integrated them."
u SLIDE 11: RESEARCH GAP
"After reviewing all this research and existing systems, the research gap becomes very clear.
Gap one - No unified platform combines project discovery and sharing with collaboration in one place. You either get 
discovery or collaboration, not both.
Gap two - Limited or no real-time communication built into the project workspace. Tools like Trello don't have chat; tools 
like Slack don't have project management.
Gap three - No skill-based or intelligent team matching for forming balanced teams. Most systems either don't have team 
formation at all, or it's manual without any intelligence.
Gap four - Weak contribution tracking and auditability for fair evaluation. GitHub has commits, but that only works for code. 
What about design work, documentation, research?
Gap five - No end-to-end project lifecycle management from idea, to team formation, to execution, to completion.
Our conclusion: No single system integrates all these features into one continuous workflow.
And that's exactly what ProjectForge aims to do."

ABSTRACT : Medical image processing is the most 
demanding and emerging field now a days. MRI is an 
advanced medical technique providing information 
about the tissue present in the brain. Brain tumor is 
dangerous disease that affects the health and life of 
human being. The conventional method of detection and 
segmentation of brain tumor is done by human 
inspection. This process is so time consuming. So to 
avoid human intervention and save time, the application 
software of brain tumor detection and segmentation is 
develop. This research paper consists of Otsu method, 
k-means , fuzzy c means and morphological operations. 
All these techniques used in this paper shown great 
potential. Otsu method and k means are used for 
segmentation whereas morphological operations 
provide a systematic approaches to analyze the 
geometric characteristics of images and are widely used 
to many applications such as edge detection, noise 
suppression etc. This method successfully detect the 
presence of brain tumor with high accuracy. 
Morphological operations provide different features 
value used in detection of brain tumor. All these factors 
helps in extracting the useful information from MRI 
images. The combination of medical and information 
technology provides great achievement in both fields. 
Keywords - MRI images, otsu’s method, k-means, fuzzy 
c-means, morphology and classification. 
I. INTRODUCTION 
Brain is the main and important part of the human body. 
So its protection is also important. Brain tumor is very 
dangerous disease. It is due to the growth of abnormal 
cells in the brain. Brain tumor can be divided into two 
parts - Primary stage tumor and secondary stage tumor. 
Primary stage tumor is also called Benign. It is not that 
much dangerous as compare to secondary stage tumor. 
Secondary stage tumor is called Malignant. Malignant 
stage is very dangerous and unrecoverable. From this 
stage it is not easy to recover back from the tumor. So to 
detect the tumor at its early stage can save human life. 
To detect the tumor one's need to go under CT scan or 
MRI. CT scan stands for Computed Tomography 
whereas MRI is Magnetic Resonance Imaging. 
This paper focus on detection of brain tumor through 
image processing. Image processing techniques are used 
to convert an image into digital image . After 
digitalization, some operations are performed to enhance 
the quality of image and at last information is extracted 
related to the signs of the brain tumor. MRI is advanced 
medical imaging technique. MRI produces high quality 
of images of the body parts. In this paper MRI images 
are processed to detect the presence of tumor. Image 
processing helps in increasing the quality of image. It 
gives the enhanced image. It treated images as a two 
dimensional signals. It convert the image into 2D by 
adjusting its pixels. Then it work on that image. Image 
processing is a rapidly growing technologies. It is an 
active research area also. The purpose of image 
processing are visualization, image sharpening and 
restoration, Image retrieval, measurement of pattern, 
Image recognition. Image processing currently works in 
Remote sensing, Intelligent transportation system, 
Defense surveillance, biomedical imaging techniques 
etc. This paper is focused on detection of brain tumor 
with high accuracy by processing the MRI images. This

PROPOSED WORK 
The proposed phase methodology for detecting brain 
tumor follows four steps. These are image 
preprocessing, segmentation, feature extraction and 
classification. In proposed work, main emphasis is on 
the segmentation of the MRI images. This segmentation 
is done by combining three approaches (otsu , k means 
and fuzzy c means). This is done so to clearly see the 
tumor region. This is the main step of the proposed 
work. The complete flow diagram of proposed work is 
given in fig 1. 
A. Image pre processing 
 
MRI scan images are taken as and these MRI images are 
stored in dataset with the size of 200*200 pixels size. 
These images are stored in JPEG, jpg or png format. The 
entries of a gray scale image are ranging from 0 to 255, 
where 0 represents black color and 255 represents pure 
white color. In this phase image quality has improved. 
The finer details in the image has also improved and 
noise is removed from the image. Most commonly used 
enhancement and noise reduction techniques are used 
for the best results. This improved and enhanced image 
will help in detecting edges and improving the quality of 
the image. Edge detection helps in finding the exact 
location of tumor. Following steps are done in the 
preprocessing stage: 
1) The input MRI image, taken from dataset is converted 
into gray scale image of size 200*200 pixels size. 
2) Image is processed to remove any noise present in the 
image 
3) Then this high quality and noiseless image undergo 
some operation to detect the edges of the 
image. 
4) The obtained image is then added to original image 
for further enhancement. 
 
B. Segmentation: 
Image segmentation is the process of dividing the image 
into small pieces. It divide the image into regions. 
Division is done on the basis of similar attributes. The 
goal of segmentation is to represent the image into more 
meaningful image that gets easier in analysis of tumor. It 
extracts important features of image. It is a challenging 
task in the field of medical imaging. In this research, 
main emphasis is on segmentation. This proposed work 
do segmentation with the help of three segmentation 
methods. 
 Otsu segmentation method 
 Segmentation using k means 
 Fuzzy c means 
Three methods are used for segmentation because they 
together give the better and clear segmented image. This 
is shown in given figure. Single method don’t give the 
clear segmented image. So there is a need to combine 
these three methods. K-means divide the image into a 
fixed number of clusters. Otsu’s thresholding divides the 
image into two classes of regions namely foreground and 
background.

Abstract 
 
Brain tumour is an irregular development by cells imitating among them in an unstoppable way. Specific identification of size and area 
of Brain tumour assumes a fundamental part in the analysis of tumour. Image processing is a dynamic research territory in which pro-
cessing of image in medical field is an exceedingly difficult field. Segmentation of image assumes a critical part in handling of image as 
it helps in the finding of suspicious districts from the restorative image. In this paper a proficient algorithm is proposed for detection of 
tumour based on segmentation of brain by means of clustering technique. The main idea in this clustering algorithm is to transfer a given 
gray-level image and then separate tumour objects position from other items of an MR image by using K-means clustering. Experiments 
say that segmentation for MR brain images can be done to help medical professionals to identify exactly size and region of the tumour 
located area in brain. 
 
Keywords: Segmentation, clustering, Brain tumour, k-means, Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)

u SLIDE 4: INTRODUCTION (Problems)
"Let me start by talking about something we've all experienced as engineering students.
Academic projects are a core part of our education - they help us apply theoretical knowledge to solve real-world problems. 
But if we're honest, the process is often quite chaotic.
First, the process is largely unstructured. Finding the right project idea, forming a team, and coordinating work takes a lot of 
time that could be spent on actual development.
Students face several common issues:
One - Limited visibility into what projects are available and what skills they require. We often don't know what our 
classmates are working on or what opportunities exist.
Two - Team formation usually happens based on friendship or convenience rather than complementary skills. We end up in 
teams with our friends, which is great socially, but might not be optimal for the project's success.
Three - Collaboration is scattered across multiple applications. We use WhatsApp for chat, Google Drive for files, Trello for 
tasks, email for formal communication - this constant context switching reduces efficiency and things often get lost.
Four - There's very low transparency of individual contributions. When evaluation time comes, it's difficult for guides to 
assess who did what, and this sometimes leads to disputes within teams.
The result? Students often remain unaware of their peers' ongoing work, miss collaboration opportunities, and spend more 
time on coordination than on actual development."
u SLIDE 5: INTRODUCTION (Solution)
To address these challenges, we propose ProjectForge, a unified, project-centric platform designed to streamline the entire 
academic project lifecycle.
It enables students to discover project opportunities, form teams based on relevant skills, and collaborate within a 
centralized workspace.
The platform brings structure, visibility, and coordination into an otherwise fragmented process.
u SLIDE 6: PROBLEM STATEMENT
"Let me now formalize the problem statement.
Challenge one - No centralized platform exists for posting and discovering academic projects within an institution.
Challenge two - Difficulty finding teammates with complementary skills and aligned interests, leading to skill-mismatched 
teams.
Challenge three - Collaboration is fragmented across multiple tools like WhatsApp, email, Google Drive, and Trello, reducing 
efficiency and clarity.
Challenge four - Lack of transparency in individual contributions, which creates confusion during evaluation and can lead to 
grade disputes.
Challenge five - Inefficient project tracking over a semester. Guides struggle to monitor task completion, deadlines, and 
progress updates across different teams.
The impact of these challenges? Students often remain unaware of peers' ongoing work, interesting ideas, and opportunities 
to collaborate. This isolation reduces innovation and limits the learning experience.
Our project aims to solve all of these challenges systematically."

Essentially, we

Q: "How will you handle security and authentication?"
"We're using JWT - JSON Web Tokens - for authentication. When a user 
logs in, they receive a token that they include with every request. 
The server validates this token to ensure the user is authenticated. 
Passwords are stored using bcrypt hashing, never in plain text."
Q: "What if the team is already full when someone tries to join?"
"That's a good concern. We're planning to implement proper validation 
on the backend. Before accepting a team member, the system checks the 
current team size against the maximum allowed. If it's full, the user 
gets a clear error message saying 'Team is full.' We're also considering 
MongoDB transactions to prevent race conditions."
Q: "How is this different from just using WhatsApp and Google Docs?"
"The key difference is integration and purpose. WhatsApp is great for 
chatting, but you can't track tasks there. Google Docs is good for files, 
but no team matching. ProjectForge brings everything together - discovery, 
formation, communication, tasks, and tracking - in one place built 
specifically for academic projects. It also adds the intelligence layer 
of skill-based matching that general tools don't have.

"So let me now present our proposed system - the ProjectForge platform.
Our system provides:
A centralized platform with structured project listings and search
Skill-based team matching using similarity scoring
Real-time communication through dedicated team channels
Task management using a Kanban-style workflow
Analytics dashboard for tracking progress and contributions
All these features are integrated into a single platform.
u SLIDE 13: APPLICATION/USE CASES
ProjectForge can be used in multiple scenarios:
Final Year Projects: for structured team formation and tracking
Hackathons: for quick team building and collaboration
Project Repository: to store and explore past work
Skill-based Networking: to connect students with similar interests
This makes it useful beyond just academic projects.
u SLIDE 14: EXPECTED DELIVERABLES
"We've divided our development into two phases to ensure quality and systematic implementation.
Phase 1 - Core Platform focuses on the foundation.
Deliverable one - User registration and login with JWT-based authentication for security.
Deliverable two - Profile creation and skill setup, which is essential for our team formation algorithm.
Deliverable three - Project posting and browsing with basic search and filter functionality.
Deliverable four - Applications and invitations for team formation. Students can apply to projects, and project owners can 
invite students.
Deliverable five - A basic project workspace UI showing project details and team members.
Phase 2 - Collaboration adds the advanced features.
Deliverable one - Real-time project and team chat using Socket.io technology, with complete message history stored in the 
database.
Deliverable two - Task management with a Kanban board interface - To-do, In-progress, and Done columns with drag-and-
drop functionality.
Deliverable three - Skill-based matching and recommendations using what we call 'Jaccard-style scoring' - essentially 
calculating skill overlap between students and project requirements.
Deliverable four - Analytics dashboard showing project progress percentages and contribution indicators for each team 
member.
Deliverable five - File sharing and attachments support so teams can share documents, designs, and resources within the 
platform.
This phased approach ensures we deliver a working core system first, then enhance it with collaboration features."
u SLIDE 15: EXPECTED OUTCOMES
"What do we expect to achieve with ProjectForge?
Outcome one - Improved collaboration through a single shared workspace where chat, tasks, and updates all happen 
together. No more 'Did you see my message on WhatsApp?' or 'Where's that file?'
Outcome two - Faster and more skill-balanced team formation using structured profiles and intelligent matching. Instead of 
spending a week finding teammates, students can form teams in a day based on actual skill fit.
Outcome three - Better project quality due to clearer planning, task tracking, and accountability. When everyone knows 
what they need to do and deadlines are visible, projects run smoother.
Outcome four - Increased innovation by improving discovery of ideas and peer work. When students can see what others are 
building, they get inspired, can offer suggestions, or even collaborate across teams.
Outcome five - Centralized knowledge sharing. Project listings, requirements, and progress are visible to guides and 
coordinators, making it easier to provide guidance and evaluate fairly.
Ultimately, ProjectForge aims to make academic projects less about logistics and more about learning and innovation."

The proposed phase methodology for detecting brain

A. Image pre processing 
 
MRI scan images are taken as and these MRI images are

These images are stored in JPEG, jpg or png format. The 
entries of a gray scale image are ranging from 0 to 255, 
where 0 represents black color and 255 represents pure 
white color. In this phase image quality has improved. 
The finer details in the image has also improved and

for the best results. This improved and enhanced image

the image. Edge detection helps in finding the exact 
location of tumor. Following steps are done in the 
preprocessing stage:

image 
3) Then this high quality and noiseless image undergo 
some operation to detect the edges of the 
image. 
4) The obtained image is then added to original image 
for further enhancement.

into small pieces. It divide the image into regions.

goal of segmentation is to represent the image into more 
meaningful image that gets easier in analysis of tumor. It 
extracts important features of image. It is a challenging 
task in the field of medical imaging. In this research, 
main emphasis is on segmentation. This proposed work 
do segmentation with the help of three segmentation 
methods.

together give the better and clear segmented image. This

clear segmented image. So there is a need to combine 
these three methods. K-means divide the image into a

u SLIDE 12: PROPOSED SYSTEM
"So let me now present our proposed system - the ProjectForge platform.
Our system provides:
A centralized platform with structured project listings and search
Skill-based team matching using similarity scoring
Real-time communication through dedicated team channels
Task management using a Kanban-style workflow
Analytics dashboard for tracking progress and contributions
All these features are integrated into a single platform.
u SLIDE 13: APPLICATION/USE CASES
ProjectForge can be used in multiple scenarios:
Final Year Projects: for structured team formation and tracking
Hackathons: for quick team building and collaboration
Project Repository: to store and explore past work
Skill-based Networking: to connect students with similar interests
This makes it useful beyond just academic projects.
u SLIDE 14: EXPECTED DELIVERABLES
"We've divided our development into two phases to ensure quality and systematic implementation.
Phase 1 - Core Platform focuses on the foundation.
Deliverable one - User registration and login with JWT-based authentication for security.
Deliverable two - Profile creation and skill setup, which is essential for our team formation algorithm.
Deliverable three - Project posting and browsing with basic search and filter functionality.
Deliverable four - Applications and invitations for team formation. Students can apply to projects, and project owners can 
invite students.
Deliverable five - A basic project workspace UI showing project details and team members.
Phase 2 - Collaboration adds the advanced features.
Deliverable one - Real-time project and team chat using Socket.io technology, with complete message history stored in the 
database.
Deliverable two - Task management with a Kanban board interface - To-do, In-progress, and Done columns with drag-and-
drop functionality.
Deliverable three - Skill-based matching and recommendations using what we call 'Jaccard-style scoring' - essentially 
calculating skill overlap between students and project requirements.
Deliverable four - Analytics dashboard showing project progress percentages and contribution indicators for each team 
member.
Deliverable five - File sharing and attachments support so teams can share documents, designs, and resources within the 
platform.
This phased approach ensures we deliver a working core system first, then enhance it with collaboration features."
u SLIDE 15: EXPECTED OUTCOMES
"What do we expect to achieve with ProjectForge?
Outcome one - Improved collaboration through a single shared workspace where chat, tasks, and updates all happen 
together. No more 'Did you see my message on WhatsApp?' or 'Where's that file?'
Outcome two - Faster and more skill-balanced team formation using structured profiles and intelligent matching. Instead of 
spending a week finding teammates, students can form teams in a day based on actual skill fit.
Outcome three - Better project quality due to clearer planning, task tracking, and accountability. When everyone knows 
what they need to do and deadlines are visible, projects run smoother.
Outcome four - Increased innovation by improving discovery of ideas and peer work. When students can see what others are 
building, they get inspired, can offer suggestions, or even collaborate across teams.
Outcome five - Centralized knowledge sharing. Project listings, requirements, and progress are visible to guides and 
coordinators, making it easier to provide guidance and evaluate fairly.
Ultimately, ProjectForge aims to make academic projects less about logistics and more about learning and innovation."

Q: "How will you handle security and authentication?"

gets a clear error message saying 'Team is full.' We're also considering 
MongoDB transactions to prevent race conditions."

chatting, but you can't track tasks there. Google Docs is good for files, 
but no team matching. ProjectForge brings everything together - discovery, 
formation, communication, tasks, and tracking - in one place built 
specifically for academic projects. It also adds the intelligence layer 
of skill-based matching that general tools don't have.

u SLIDE 8: LITERATURE SURVEY (Part 1)
"For our literature survey, we reviewed 15 papers and systems across multiple categories - team matching, collaboration 
platforms, project management systems, and related technologies.
Let me walk you through some key findings.
Paper one - 'Personalized Group-Based Recommendation' from 2018 discusses group recommender systems, but it's 
designed for general item recommendations, not specifically for academic team formation.
Paper two - A web-based learning system from 2022 enables student interaction but lacks project workflow management.
Paper three - The Collaborative Filtering Survey from 2009 gives us recommender system techniques but again, not specific 
to academic projects.
[Move down the table]
Papers four and five - Node.js Project Management System and DBIT Portal - both provide centralized project tracking but 
lack intelligent matching algorithms.
[Look at audience]
The pattern we see here? Existing research focuses on either team formation or project management, but not both in an 
integrated way."
[Click to next slide]
u SLIDE 9: LITERATURE SURVEY (Part 2)
"Moving to the next set of papers...
Papers six through eleven cover various aspects - e-learning platforms, agile methodologies, project sharing systems, 
analytics dashboards, and security models.
Paper six - IEEE E-learning platform provides structured digital learning but no project collaboration features.
Paper seven - Agile methodology study discusses Scrum and Kanban workflows, which influenced our task management 
design, but it's a process framework, not a platform solution.
Paper eight - Student Project Sharing Platform from 2024 is actually quite close to what we're building, but it has limited 
advanced features and no intelligent team matching.
Papers nine, ten, and eleven focus on analytics, file storage, and security - all important components, but none address the 
complete project lifecycle we're targeting.
What's interesting is that these papers validate that pieces of our solution exist in research, but no one has integrated them."
u SLIDE 11: RESEARCH GAP
"After reviewing all this research and existing systems, the research gap becomes very clear.
Gap one - No unified platform combines project discovery and sharing with collaboration in one place. You either get 
discovery or collaboration, not both.
Gap two - Limited or no real-time communication built into the project workspace. Tools like Trello don't have chat; tools 
like Slack don't have project management.
Gap three - No skill-based or intelligent team matching for forming balanced teams. Most systems either don't have team 
formation at all, or it's manual without any intelligence.
Gap four - Weak contribution tracking and auditability for fair evaluation. GitHub has commits, but that only works for code. 
What about design work, documentation, research?
Gap five - No end-to-end project lifecycle management from idea, to team formation, to execution, to completion.
Our conclusion: No single system integrates all these features into one continuous workflow.
And that's exactly what ProjectForge aims to do."
